1-genital-changes.explain: The non-pregnant uterus weighs 50 g and increases to 1000 g at term.

1-genital-changes.q: The uterine volume at term is approximately:
1-genital-changes.explain: Uterine volume increases from 10 mL (non-pregnant) to 5000 mL at term.

1-genital-changes.q: Myometrial hypertrophy is primarily due to:
1-genital-changes.explain: Hypertrophy of the myometrium is an estrogenic effect, while hyperplasia is a progesterone effect.

1-genital-changes.q: The lower uterine segment starts to form at:
1-genital-changes.explain: The lower uterine segment (from isthmus + lower 1/2 inch of uterine body) starts forming at 12 weeks and reaches 10 cm at full term.

options: Painless contractions that may cause discomfort late in pregnancy
Contractions that only occur in multiple pregnancies
1-genital-changes.explain: Braxton Hicks are irregular, painless contractions that cause discomfort late in pregnancy and may account for false labor pains.

1-genital-changes.q: Uterine blood flow at term reaches approximately:
1-genital-changes.explain: Uterine artery lumen is doubled and blood flow increases to about 500 mL/min at term.

2-hematological-changes.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
2-hematological-changes.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

2-hematological-changes.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
2-hematological-changes.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

2-hematological-changes.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
2-hematological-changes.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

2-hematological-changes.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
2-hematological-changes.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.
